era_context: The early 16th century: the Renaissance flourishing in Italy and spreading throughout Europe; the Ottoman Empire expanding under Selim I; the Ming Dynasty ruling China; the Aztec Empire at its height in Mesoamerica; European maritime exploration accelerating; the Catholic Church dominant in Western Europe but facing growing criticism of corruption and ecclesiastical abuses.

events.title: Ferdinand of Aragon dies; Charles V becomes heir to Spanish throne
events.description: Ferdinand II of Aragon, the co-ruler of Spain (along with his deceased wife Isabella of Castile), dies on January 23, 1516. Ferdinand's death marks the end of an era in Spanish history. His grandson, Charles of Ghent (later known as Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor), becomes the heir to the Spanish throne. Charles, who was born in 1500 and raised in the Low Countries, inherits the Spanish kingdoms of Castile and Aragon, as well as the vast Spanish colonial possessions in the Americas and elsewhere. Charles will become one of the most powerful rulers in Europe, eventually inheriting the Holy Roman Empire in 1519. The succession of Charles V marks a significant shift in European politics and the beginning of Spanish dominance in European affairs.

sources.name: Brandi, Karl. The Emperor Charles V, 1939

events.certainty_note: Ferdinand of Aragon's death on January 23, 1516 is confirmed by contemporary Spanish records and modern scholarship. Charles's succession as heir to the Spanish throne is well-documented.
cross_references: 1519-001 (Cortés's expedition to Mexico, authorized under Charles V's rule)

events.title: Battle of Ridaniyya (Marj al-Dabiq); Ottoman victory over Mamluk Sultanate
events.description: On August 24, 1516, the Ottoman forces under Selim I defeat the Mamluk Sultanate in the Battle of Ridaniyya (also known as Marj al-Dabiq), fought near Aleppo in northern Syria. The Ottoman victory is decisive, resulting in the death of the Mamluk Sultan Qansuh al-Ghawri and the capture of Aleppo. The battle marks a turning point in the Ottoman-Mamluk conflict, establishing Ottoman military superiority and paving the way for the Ottoman conquest of Egypt and the Levant. The victory is facilitated by Ottoman military innovations, including the use of gunpowder weapons and artillery, which give the Ottomans an advantage over the Mamluk cavalry-based forces. The battle leads to the rapid collapse of Mamluk power and the expansion of the Ottoman Empire.
key_figures: Qansuh al-Ghawri (Mamluk Sultan, killed in battle)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ridaniyya on August 24, 1516 is confirmed by both Ottoman and Mamluk sources. The date, location, and outcome are well-established in contemporary records and modern scholarship.

events.title: Desiderius Erasmus publishes the Greek New Testament
events.description: Desiderius Erasmus, the Dutch humanist scholar, publishes the Novum Instrumentum omne (later known as the Textus Receptus), a Greek edition of the New Testament with Latin translation and commentary. This work represents a major achievement in Renaissance scholarship and biblical studies. Erasmus's edition is based on the best available Greek manuscripts and represents an attempt to establish the most accurate text of the New Testament. The publication of Erasmus's Greek New Testament has profound implications for the Protestant Reformation, as it provides reformers like Martin Luther with access to the original Greek text of the New Testament, enabling them to challenge the authority of the Latin Vulgate and the interpretations of the Catholic Church. Erasmus's work exemplifies the humanist emphasis on returning to original sources and the application of philological methods to classical and biblical texts.

sources.name: Rummel, Erika. Erasmus and His Catholic Critics, 1989

events.certainty_note: The publication of Erasmus's Greek New Testament in 1516 is confirmed by contemporary records and modern scholarship. The date and significance of this work are well-established.
cross_references: 1517-001 (Luther's Ninety-Five Theses, influenced by humanist scholarship)

events.title: Ludovico Ariosto publishes Orlando Furioso; major work of Renaissance literature
events.description: Ludovico Ariosto, an Italian poet, publishes the first edition of Orlando Furioso (The Frenzy of Orlando), an epic poem that becomes one of the masterpieces of Renaissance literature. The work, published in 1516 and revised in subsequent editions, tells the story of the knight Orlando and his adventures during the reign of Charlemagne. Orlando Furioso is characterized by its complex narrative structure, vivid descriptions, and exploration of themes such as love, honor, and madness. The poem becomes widely read throughout Europe and influences subsequent European literature. Ariosto's work exemplifies the achievements of the Italian Renaissance in literature and represents the height of the epic poem tradition in European literature.

sources.name: Javitch, Daniel. Proclaiming a Classic: The Canonization of Orlando Furioso, 1991

events.certainty_note: The publication of Orlando Furioso in 1516 is confirmed by contemporary records and modern scholarship. The date and significance of this work are well-established.

events.title: Thomas More publishes Utopia; influential work of political philosophy
events.description: Thomas More, an English humanist and statesman, publishes Utopia, a satirical work of political philosophy that describes an ideal society on a fictional island. The work, written in Latin and published in 1516, critiques contemporary European society and explores ideas about justice, equality, and the organization of society. Utopia becomes an influential work in European intellectual history, inspiring subsequent utopian and dystopian literature and political philosophy. More's work exemplifies the humanist engagement with classical learning and the application of humanist ideas to contemporary political and social issues. The term 'utopia' becomes a standard term in political discourse, referring to an idealized or imagined perfect society.

sources.name: Skinner, Quentin. The Foundations of Modern Political Thought, 1978

events.certainty_note: The publication of Utopia in 1516 is confirmed by contemporary records and modern scholarship. The date and significance of this work are well-established.

events.title: Montezuma II continues rule of Aztec Empire; tributary system expands
events.description: Montezuma II continues to rule the Aztec Empire in 1516, consolidating and expanding the empire's territorial control and tributary system. The Aztec Empire at this time is at the height of its power, controlling a vast network of tributary states that provide tribute in goods, labor, and sacrificial victims. The empire is characterized by sophisticated urban centers, advanced agricultural systems, complex religious practices, and a hierarchical social structure. However, the empire also faces internal tensions and resistance from tributary states. In 1516, the Aztec Empire is at the apex of its power, though it will soon face the challenge of Spanish conquest initiated by Cortés's landing in 1519.

sources.name: Sahagún, Bernardino de. Florentine Codex (Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España), compiled 1558–1582
Durán, Diego. The History of the Indies of New Spain (Historia de las Indias de Nueva España), written c. 1581

events.certainty_note: Montezuma II's rule of the Aztec Empire in 1516 is confirmed by indigenous sources and Spanish colonial records. His reign (1502–1520) is well-documented, though specific events in individual years are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Ming Dynasty continues rule of China; cultural and architectural achievements
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continues to rule China in 1516 under the Zhengde Emperor (r. 1505–1521). The Ming Dynasty is characterized by centralized imperial rule, the construction of monumental architecture such as the Forbidden City and the Great Wall, and the sponsorship of maritime exploration under Admiral Zheng He. In 1516, the Ming court continues to support cultural and architectural achievements, including the production of porcelain, the advancement of painting and calligraphy, and the compilation of scholarly works. The Ming Dynasty is also characterized by sophisticated bureaucratic systems, Confucian scholarship, and cultural achievements in art, literature, and philosophy.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records

events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's rule of China in 1516 is confirmed by official court records and modern scholarship. The Zhengde Emperor was ruling in 1516, and the dynasty's cultural and architectural achievements are well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Battle of Ridaniyya is sometimes given as August 24, 1516, but some sources place it in 1517. The conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate was a process that extended over several months, with the final conquest of Cairo occurring in January 1517, so the dating of 1516 for the battle is accurate, but the complete conquest took place in 1517. The publication date of Erasmus's Greek New Testament is sometimes given as 1516 or 1517, depending on the calendar system used (Julian vs. Gregorian). The first edition was published in March 1516 by the Basel printer Johann Froben.
historiographic_note: The year 1516 is well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding political events (Ferdinand of Aragon's death, Charles V's succession), cultural achievements (Erasmus's Greek New Testament, Ariosto's Orlando Furioso, More's Utopia), and military events (Battle of Ridaniyya). However, the historical record is heavily shaped by European perspectives. Ottoman and Mamluk sources provide information about the Battle of Ridaniyya, though these are often filtered through imperial perspectives. Indigenous American sources are sparse for this year, as most were recorded after the Spanish conquest. Chinese sources regarding the Ming Dynasty are official court records, which may omit or downplay certain activities. Overall, 1516 is a year of significant European cultural and political developments, but the historical record is unevenly distributed across regions, with Europe well-documented and other regions less thoroughly represented.

graph_edges.note: Ferdinand of Aragon's death in 1516 led to Charles V becoming Holy Roman Emperor in 1519
The Battle of Ridaniyya in 1516 led to Selim I's conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate in 1517
Erasmus's Greek New Testament in 1516 provided the textual basis for Luther's critique of the Church in 1517
Ariosto's Orlando Furioso and More's Utopia were both published in 1516, representing major Renaissance literary achievements
